data.title: Politics And The Markets 06/13/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Operating Income Improvements And Turnaround Strategy Make Newell Brands A Buy
data.description:
Newell Brands Inc. (NASDAQ:NWL) recently reported an increase in operating income as a result of improvements in productivity and recent restructuring actions. The company announced potential new initiatives to be implemented in 2025, new products in the L&D segment, and new pricing strategies. Even considering risks from new tariffs, an eventual increase in interest rates, and the actions of rating agencies, NWL appears quite undervalued. Given the total amount of free cash flow produced, in my opinion, the company will most likely trade at higher price marks in the coming years. In 2016, NWL sold shares at more than $40 per share. Right now, we can buy shares at $5-$6 per share.

Newell Brands Trades At 3.40x 2029 EPS, And 4.7x FWD Cash flow

Newell Brands Inc. reports three business segments named Home and commercial solutions, Learning and development, and Outdoor and recreation. The company has been selling consumer goods since 1903, which means a significant amount of know-how accumulated in the market. Besides, we are talking about a large corporation with close to 23700 employees and a market capitalization of $2-$3 billion.

Newell Brands didn't report better than expected EPS GAAP in the last quarter. However, I believe that there is a lot of new information that may bring the attention of new investors. Quarterly net revenue declined because of the declines in demand and issues with distribution costs. The company also appears to be existing in certain markets, which is not also helping. With that being said, NWL did note the introduction of new products in the L&D segment in combination with new pricing strategies, which may help deal with inflation in the coming quarters.

In my opinion, the most beneficial information reported was about the company's operating income. NWL appears to be making a lot of progress thanks to productivity increases and previous restructuring efforts. The fact that NWL did reduce its restructuring efforts in the last quarter indicates that the Realignment Plan may be over soon. I obtained the following words from the last quarterly report.

Operating income was $21 million as compared to $16 million in the prior year period. The improvement reflects benefits from gross productivity, savings from restructuring actions primarily related to the Realignment Plan and lower restructuring and restructuring-related charges of $14 million. Source: 10-Q

NWL appears to trade at 3.40x 2029 EPS, and 5x 2028 EPS. In addition, analysts out there are expecting 2029 EPS growth of 70%, and 2028 EPS growth of 25%.

Besides, the company appears to be trading at 10x FWD GAAP earnings, with peers trading at 18x FWD GAAP earnings. The price/ FWD cash flow ratio also stands at about 4.7x FWD cash flow. Competitors seem to trade at 10.5x FWD cash flow. In sum, I would say that the stock is undervalued.

The Ongoing Turnaround Strategy Could Push Future Financial Figures

NWL is making a number of changes that, I believe, could offer operating margin and profit margin improvements in the coming years. In combination with centralization of domestic retail sales teams and other teams, the company initiated cost reduction initiatives that took place in 2024, and will most likely have a beneficial effect in 2025 and future years.

As part of the Realignment Plan, the Company has made several operating model changes, which entailed: standing up a cross-functional brand management organization, realigning business unit finance to fully support the new global brand management model, further simplifying and standardizing regional go-to-market organizations, and centralizing domestic retail sales teams, the digital technology team, business-aligned accounting personnel, the Manufacturing Quality team, and the Human Resources functions into the appropriate center-led teams to drive standardization, efficiency and scale with a One Newell approach. Source: 10-Q

There is more. In the last quarterly report, NWL noted that the company expects to launch new actions in 2025 that will depend on consultation requirements. New information being delivered about the new actions could bring new demand from new investors.

Remaining actions, subject to applicable local law and consultation requirements, are expected to be implemented by the end of fiscal year 2025. Source: 10-Q

NWL Is Making A Lot Of Efforts To Reduce The Impact Of New Tariffs

I understand investors who decided to sell consumer companies as soon as new tariffs were announced in the United States. With that, I think that the decline in the stock price of NWL was not that logical. In the last 10-k, the company reported that total revenue in the United States is close to $4.7 billion, which is more than 60% of the total amount of global revenue. The last time the company traded at $5-$6 was in the great recession of 2008 to 2009. In my view, with the information we have today, I think that the stock price went down too much.

Besides, according the NWL, the company has a number of facilities in the United States, which will most likely benefit future operating margin improvements.

We believe that the Company is well positioned to respond to the current tariff environment, primarily because the Company maintains a significant U.S. manufacturing presence of 15 production facilities and manufactures, in the U.S. and two of its facilities in Mexico, products representing over half of the Company’s U.S. revenues that are not presently subject to the recently announced U.S. tariffs. This manufacturing presence is expected to provide a competitive advantage to the Company in certain categories where its competitors are exposed to import tariffs. Source: 10-Q

Besides, NWL seems to be implementing a number of initiatives for sourcing raw materials and finished products from a number of different countries. With this in mind, in my view, geographic diversification may protect the company from large tariff changes in certain countries.

The Company also has a scaled, centralized procurement team that is proficient in sourcing raw materials and finished products from over 50 countries around the world. Source: 10-Q

Increase In The Interest Rates, And Weighted Average Interest Rates

I am not really worried about the total amount of debt because of the total amount of free cash flow that the company produces. According to Seeking Alpha, the company reported total debt of $5.5 billion. In 2028, the company reported unlevered free cash flow of $4.134 billion.

With that being said, investors may want to research a bit the recent increases in interest expenses, which may also deteriorate net income growth in the near future. Besides, in April 2025, Moody’s lowered the company's senior debt rating to “B1”. As a result, large fund managers may decide to lower their exposition to the company, and conservative investors may decide to sell shares. These are risks to keep in mind in the coming years. If NWL cannot finance the introduction of new products, or debt investors do not offer beneficial financing conditions, in my view, future new income growth may not grow as expected.

Interest expense, net increased by $2 million due to higher interest rates and lower interest income. The weighted average interest rates for the three months ended March 31, 2025 and 2024 were approximately 6.0% and 5.6%, respectively. Source: 10-Q

On April 14, 2025, Moody’s Corporation (“Moody’s”) downgraded the Company’s senior unsecured debt rating to “B1”. Source: 10-Q

Risks

With tariffs, there are other risks that may destroy the company's future financial figures. In the last quarterly report, the company mentioned high competition in the markets, where it operates facing inflation challenges and certain macroeconomic volatility. Some of these risks are unpredictable. I also believe that NWL may not be able to protect its business model from certain global risks that may affect most companies out there, such as large changes in the interest rates and changes in foreign currency or the U.S. dollar.

The Company is implementing this strategy while continuing to address key global challenges such as shifting consumer preferences and behaviors; a highly competitive operating environment; a rapidly changing retail and consumer landscape; continued macroeconomic and geopolitical volatility; a soft macro backdrop; significant cumulative inflationary pressures on consumers; recently announced tariffs by the current U.S. presidential administration and other countries’ retaliatory actions in response to such tariffs; and an evolving regulator. Source: 10-Q

In 2024 and 2025, the company seemed to exit in certain markets, which lowered future revenue growth potential. Under the worst-case scenario, NWL may be wrong in selling certain assets or discontinuing the production of certain items. In addition, NWL may also lose valuable, talented individuals. If NWL loses certain key employees, in my opinion, future free cash flow margins may not be as good as expected. If a number of analysts note a reduction in the free cash flow margin expectations, in my opinion, the demand for the stock could decline.

The Fair Price Could Be Close To $11

For the assessment of future free cash flow, I studied quite a bit of the company's previous free cash flow. NWL reported positive free cash flow many times from 1992 to 2024, so making forecasts about the future appears easy. I assumed that the company will be able to finance future operations, and took into account potential effects of tariffs.

Besides, I took into consideration the recent introduction of products and the restructuring efforts mentioned. With FCF ranging from $1 billion in 2025 to $1.1 billion in 2035, WACC of 10%, net debt of $5.3 billion, and 417 million shares, the fair price would not be far from $11 per share. I used FCF multiple of 7x, which, in my view, appears reasonable.

My Final Words: The Company Is A Buy At 3.40x 2029 EPS

With recent increases in the quarterly operating margin, NWL appears to show the benefits of the efforts made in the last two years. Besides, NWL recently noted new restructuring actions that may be implemented by the end of fiscal year 2025, which could bring the attention of new investors. I would also expect further free cash flow growth as a result of new products in the L&D segment in combination with new pricing strategies. There are some risks coming from the total amount of debt, large changes in the interest rates, or the damages of tariffs. With that, given that the company has been operating since 1903, and reports substantial free cash flow, in my opinion, NWL will most likely operate for many more years. I do see NWL quite undervalued at 3.40x 2029 EPS.

data.title: Price Inflation Moves Up Slightly, And Wages Still Aren't Keeping Up
data.description:
By Ryan McMaken

According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics’ latest price inflation data, CPI inflation in May rose for the second month in a row.

The year-over-year change in the CPI rose by 2.4 percent in May, slightly accelerating over April’s year-over-year increase of 2.3 percent. The month-over-month change (seasonally adjusted) was positive again in May, rising 0.1 percent, although May’s increase, with the exception of March this year, was the smallest month-over-month increase since mid-2024.

Much of this was fueled by ongoing increases in the cost of shelter and services. Shelter, for instance, rose by 3.9 percent, year over year, while services (less energy services) rose by 3.6 percent.

The overall trend is one of deceleration in the CPI, but at a much slower pace than has long been predicted by Federal Reserve officials.

This same trend is found in the less volatile measure “CPI less food and energy,” also known as “core CPI.” By this measure, May’s print was up year over year by 2.8 percent. That’s unchanged from April’s increase of 2.8 percent. Month-over-month, the core CPI rose by 0.1 percent. Core CPI has not gone down month-over-month in 61 months.

Price inflation is not decelerating as quickly as central bankers had hoped. Although May numbers have not yet been published, the Fed’s preferred inflation measure, PCE inflation, printed at 2.5 percent in April, seven months after the FOMC cut the target policy rate, declaring that price inflation would soon fall to the Fed’s arbitrary two-percent goal. With May’s upward tick in CPI and core CPI numbers, May’s PCE reading may be an eighth month of uncooperative price inflation levels.

We are likely to see some downward pressure on price inflation in coming months, however, since there is increasing evidence that Americans have less disposable income to spend. Home listings are hitting multi-year highs, but far fewer buyers appear interested in purchasing them.

Not surprisingly, home prices are beginning to fall, but we appear to be only at the very beginning of this trend. We have yet to see any substantial declines in rents yet, but indications point to an overall slowing in shelter prices in CPI measures.

Other indicators of stagnating or falling prices can be found in the fact that delinquencies on credit cards, auto loans, and student loans are all at or above the highest levels reported since the Great Recession. This will all cut discretionary spending and put downward pressure on prices in some areas.

It should be noted that even with the economy slowing, we are not seeing price inflation return to truly stable - that is, zero level - price inflation levels. Price inflation continues to move upward, and it cannot be said that price inflation has actually gone down in any meaningful sense. It has only decelerated.

Indeed, if we look at price inflation since the beginning of 2021, as the economy emerged from the covid lockdowns of 2020, the CPI has increased by 22 percent. Anyone who hasn’t experienced a wage increase of at least 22 percent over that period is now poorer.

This is indeed the case for many Americans, since average hourly earnings have increased by only 21 percent in that time. In other words, growth in average earnings has not been enough to keep up with the CPI.

Unfortunately, the average American is unlikely to experience much relief from this trend any time soon. As the economy worsens, the Fed will be pressured to cut the target interest rate so as to “stimulate” the economy.

At this point in the business cycle, another interest rate cut is unlikely to do much to prevent continued economy stagnation. Rather, an embrace of even more dovish monetary policy will simply set the stage for building the next boom-bust cycle and prevent badly needed price deflation.

Meanwhile, ordinary people are likely to see more stagnation in real wages, or worse, they may face rising unemployment.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: National Grid: On Course To Invest In Energy Transition By 2029 (Hold)
data.description:
Shares of global electricity utility company National Grid Plc. (NYSE:NGG) has surged 25.97% (YoY). The company announced its FY 2024/2025 earnings results in mid-May 2025 which saw its operating profit jump 12% (YoY) despite the 7.42% (YoY) decline in gross revenue. I will explain why this stock is a hold after it completes the sale of its US onshore renewables business, to grow its reinvestment strategy, especially in electric assets, by 2029 and expand its energy infrastructure. However, NGG’s reduced revenue in the FY 2024/2025 was attributed to the sale of its “Electricity System Operator” portfolio to the UK government back in late 2024, which may be translated to the recent sale of its US renewables business in 2025.

National Grid Plc announced the completion of the sale of its US onshore renewables business at the end of May 2025 for $1.7 billion to Brookfield Asset Management (BAM). Over the past year, NGG has outperformed the S&P500 by about 16% (YoY) while BAM has outperformed the S&P500 by about 34% (YoY).

National Grid is focused on transforming its business empire into electricity, with about 80% of its assets expected to be electric by 2029. It sold its gas/ metering business, and LNG assets in the UK in 2024, and it indicated its intention to complete the sale of the entire NGG US onshore renewables business by H1 2026. Further, NGG is acting by the UK's Energy Act of 2023 in, among others, the sale of the Energy System Operator in 2024 that will go a long way in preventing the nationalization of the company in the future. Additionally, we are looking at a company that intends to invest about£60 billion towards its energy networks in the US/ UK by 2029 (which is about 3.3 more than its FY 2024/2025 revenue and about 6 times more than its current capital investment of £9.8 billion made in that fiscal year). The company also expects to raise its group’s asset base to over £100 billion by March 2029 after its regulated asset growth (RAV) in FY 2024/2025 reached 10.5% from 9.1% in the prior year.

Valuation

In valuation, I will use the net asset value per share method, since National Grid is an asset-heavy company. It has a market capitalization of $68.89 billion with energy network assets spread across the UK and the US. I will also consider the PE ratio of NGG to get a clearer picture of its share price against its earnings potential.

As of March 31, 2025; NGG’s asset base stood at £106.742 billion against total liabilities of£68.916 billion, leaving off a net asset value of£37.826 billion ($51.32 billion). NGG has about 4.9 billion outstanding share balance. It means that its net asset value per share count is $11.16 against the current share price of $71.47 (a difference of 540%). Thus, the market values NGG’s potential earnings higher and the company is trading at a premium in comparison to its book value. Further, NGG’s forward price-to-earnings (PE) ratio is 16.02X against the forward EPS of 5.01. This computation gives a future price of $80.26, indicating a difference of +12.3% (YoY).

Brookfield Asset Management has a total asset base of $14.966 billion against total liabilities of $4.073 billion, leaving off $10.893 billion in net asset value. BAM has an outstanding share balance of 1.613 billion, meaning that its net asset value per share is $6.75. It represents a difference of 726.37% from the current share price (as of this writing). So both NGG and BAM are trading at a premium in comparison to their book values as far as their net asset values are concerned. Both companies have a positive net asset value. When we consider the PE ratio, BAM has a forward PE of 33.78X and a forward EPS of 1.65. This computation gives 55.74, a difference of -0.07% (YoY).

In addition, over the past 4 years, NGG recorded an average dividend yield of 5.3% against the current 4.30% (TTM). It is 25% more than the sector median of 3.45%. NGG is also expected to grow its cash balance following the sale of its US onshore renewables business that will have a positive impact on dividend payouts into 2026.

I will expand my comparison to include other utility companies such as Engie SA (OTCPK:ENGIY), which is up 45.40% (YoY), Sempra (SRE) at +0.41% (YoY), and Dominion Energy (D) at +7.84% (YoY).

The three stocks: ENGIY, D, and SRE are trading at their fair values, indicating a positive reflection of the company’s intrinsic worth as far as the net asset values are concerned.

Risks

National Grid realized a 7.42% (YoY) revenue decline in the FY 2024/2025, with the reduction mainly attributed to its sale of the “Electricity System Operator" which is now the National Electricity System Operator of the UK government. We may witness a similar decline in the FY 2025/2026 after the sale of the US onshore renewables business. There was also a 4% (YoY) decline in the cash generated from NGG’s continued operations. The group’s return on equity (RoE) also fell by 150 basis points to 9.0% in the FY 2024/2025.

It also recorded an impairment of £303 million ($411.71 million) due to a pause in its US offshore wind business. The company only sold its US onshore renewables business, leaving behind the offshore business that continues to face challenges in 2025. This impairment may affect NGG’s revenue into 2 026.

Bottom Line

I have rated National Grid as a hold given its expanding reinvestment strategy in the electric business after it divested its US onshore business. However, the divestment may cause revenue decline into 2026 as it also faces an impairment concern in its US offshore business. In terms of valuation, the market values NGG’s potential earnings higher and the company is trading at a premium in comparison to its book value.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Deutsche Post: Long-Term Buy Despite Tariffs Uncertainties
data.description:
Company Description

DHL Group/Deutsche Post (DHL Group) (OTCPK:DHLGY) (OTCPK:DPSTF) is a Germany-based multinational and a global leader in postal services, express courier, logistics, and supply chain management. With operations in over 220 countries and territories, the group is structured around five core business divisions:

  • Express (29.4% of revenue): International delivery of time-sensitive documents and parcels.
  • Global Forwarding & Freight (22.9%): Freight forwarding via air, ocean, and road transport.
  • Supply Chain (20.1%): Contract logistics, including warehousing, transportation, and value-added services.
  • eCommerce Solutions (7.5%): Tailored shipping services for online retailers.
  • Post & Parcel Germany (20.1%): Domestic mail and parcel delivery within Germany.

From a geographical perspective, over 50% of group revenues are generated in Europe, with the Americas accounting for just above 25%.

Stock Performance and recent developments

Over the last 12 months, DHL shares have traded in a range between EUR 32.6 and EUR 44.3. Volatility peaked around April 2nd (Liberation Day), following the U.S. administration’s announcement of higher import tariffs, which sparked fears of a broader downturn in global trade. However, subsequent easing in rhetoric and policy direction contributed to a rebound in the share price.

The stock has delivered a total return of over 25.5% year-to-date, outperforming the DAX index, which gained 19% over the same period.

The stock rebound has been reinforced by solid Q1 ‘25 results. Revenue rose 2.8% y/y to EUR 20.8 bn, in line with market expectations, EBIT by 4.5% to EUR 1.37 bn and EPS by 7.9% to EUR 0.68 (+5.8% vs consensus).

The management reiterated FY ‘25 guidance for an EBIT ≥ EUR 6 billion and a Free Cash Flow of approximately EUR 3 billion.

Investment case

Deutsche Post AG has navigated a challenging macroeconomic environment over the past two years, following the post-COVID surge in demand during 2021 and 2022. The normalization of global trade volumes and rising cost pressures due to inflation have weighed on profitability. Between 2022 and 2024, revenues declined by 10.8%, while EBIT contracted by 48%, driving EBIT margins down from 8.9% to 6.7%.

However, as indicated previously, Q1 2025 results signaled a potential inflection point, with both revenue and EBIT returning to growth, indicating a stabilization of the core business.

We believe the Company presents an attractive medium- to long-term investment opportunity, particularly for investors seeking lower volatility relative to equity indices and reliable dividend income.

The DHL Group investment case is based on two pillars:

1) The “DHL Group Strategy 2030”, presented on September 24, sets out the company’s roadmap through the end of the decade. Management is targeting >5% annual revenue growth, implying a +50% increase in revenues by 2030 vs. 2023 levels and a strong Free Cash Flow generation to support value creation through disciplined reinvestment and a sharper focus on Return on Invested Capital (ROIC). Growth initiatives are tailored to each business segment, with Life Sciences identified as a key driver. The segment generated EUR 5 billion in revenue and is expected to double over the next five years, supported by increasing demand for specialized logistics in healthcare and pharmaceuticals. Another key growth driver is Global eCommerce. While the CAGR forecast of 19.2% through 2030 (as per end-2024 estimates) may be too optimistic in a scenario of rising import tariff, growth could remain robust.

2) The Company offers a 4.5% dividend yield, which investors could consider attractive relative to the current 10-year German government bond yield (2.5%), particularly in a low-rate environment supported by the ECB’s accommodative stance. The company’s payout policy of 40–60% leaves room for dividend growth, underpinned by improving profitability and a projected decline in capex requirements in the medium term.

Risks

The principal downside risk remains a slowdown in global trade, particularly if geopolitical or tariffs-related tensions escalate. While DHL has introduced customer mitigation strategies (highlighted during Q1 2025 earnings), these may not fully offset macro-level headwinds.

A weaker U.S. dollar could also negatively impact earnings, given the Group’s international exposure and currency translation effects. At the end of 2004, the company held derivative contracts hedging the EUR/USD exchange rate at 1.09. Given that both revenues and costs are largely denominated in the same local currencies, the impact of exchange rate fluctuations is mostly limited to the translation of foreign earnings into euros. Based on current exchange rates, the USD at 1.15 could have a modest negative impact on FY 2025 EPS, estimated at around 1–2%.

Valuation

At the current share price of EUR41 and FY2025 expected EPS of EUR 3.00, DHL trades at 13.7x forward earnings, below its historical average of 14.9x, suggesting modest undervaluation. A reversion to the long-term average would imply a price increase to EUR 50.6/share by 2026 end, reflecting a potential 24% upside.

The current dividend yield of 4.5% is above the long-term average of 3.8%, further supporting the valuation case.

Under our base case scenario, we estimate a 12.3% total annual return through 2030, based on:

  • 5% annual EPS growth (aligned with revenue guidance),
  • Valuation multiple expansion back toward historical averages,
  • Dividend yield of 4.5%.

This return profile supports our Buy rating on the stock.

The view from the street

Despite strong year-to-date performance, the analysts’ sentiment remains constructive: out of 19 analysts covering Deutsche Post, 10 have a Buy recommendation on the stock, 8 a Hold recommendation and only 1 analyst suggests selling it. Q1 2025 results have helped stabilize EPS and price target revisions, breaking a prior trend of downward adjustments.

Conclusion

Despite the strong year-to-date rally, we believe that Deutsche Post continues to represent an attractive buying opportunity, supported by its long-term structural growth potential and an attractive dividend yield. While current fundamentals remain supportive, the investment case could be challenged in the event of a sudden deterioration in global trade policy dynamics.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ladder Capital: High Yield, Low Leverage - A REIT Navigating CRE Headwinds
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome The Payout Desk as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

Ladder Capital Corp (NYSE:LADR) remains one of the more attractive commercial mortgage REITs, with its above-average dividend yield and conservatively capitalized balance sheet, as the commercial real estate (CRE) macro headwinds persist. Ladder Capital is an internally managed, middle-market focused, commercial real estate finance REIT with a diversified platform across originating senior secured first mortgage loans, owning net-leased commercial properties, and investing in high-quality MBS. Ladder's multipronged business model affords it several streams of revenue and optionality to flex its exposure to CRE markets.

A 9% dividend yield in this market typically tends to indicate one of two things: either it’s a trap — or it’s a mispricing of a rare opportunity. I see LADR as resolutely in the latter category. There is no rodeo riding, debt-fueled growth vehicle here, and certainly no melting ice cube that’s burning cash. This is a conservatively managed CRE lender with low leverage, ample liquidity, a management team that owns >10% of total equity, and a broad market scorn of all things CRE, which has created an opening for a steady income stream, priced at a discount, backed by real assets and real cash flow. I’m long, and I think upside from here is too good to resist.

Key Thesis Drivers

Conservative Balance Sheet Underpins Dividend Safety

Ladder has a strong balance sheet relative to peers, providing strong dividend coverage and resilience. Adjusted leverage is only ~1.4x vs. ~3x peer averages. ~98% of debt is non-mark-to-market and the Ladder is 72% unsecured bonds, limiting the risk of needing to refinance or meet margin calls. This solid financing structure allowed Ladder to preserve book value over the last year, undepreciated book value per share ~$13.66, flat year over year, while peers declined ~17%. With a current ratio of ~16.8× and an Altman Z-score of 7.5 (strong), liquidity ($1.5 billion available) provides a good buffer to maintain operations and dividends. In other words, Ladder's low leverage and strong liquidity position support a 1.4x dividend coverage on distributable earnings.

Valuation Gap: Shareholder Yield Optionality

Ladder is currently trading at a significant discount to intrinsic value, with a current price-to-book (P/B) ratio of ~0.7, below its historical average. Market sentiment appears to be pricing in a bearish CRE outlook, given how higher rates and concerns over CRE valuations (particularly for office) have depressed the entire sector. Ladder's portfolio mix and balance sheet, however, should be more resilient to these pressures than most peers.

Ladder's ~$4 + billion asset portfolio is largely split between first lien mortgage loans (LADR's loan book is >99% senior secured mortgages & average LTV is a modest ~66%) and real estate equity (commercial property investments, many of which are net-leased to high-credit tenants), with a substantial securities portfolio (~$2 billion), which is almost all investment-grade rated bonds. This yields a defensive, income-heavy asset mix, and a diversified revenue mix that should buffer Ladder's earnings vs. a CRE downturn relative to a pure-play lender. Ladder also runs at well below typical mREIT leverage (~1.6× debt/equity as of early '25) & funds with mostly long-term unsecured debt (NOT with repo or warehouse lines), which helped it achieve the distinction of being the only commercial mREIT with an IG credit rating. Simply put, Ladder's "high yield, low leverage" positioning is actually backed up by real balance sheet credit quality.

There are several potential catalysts that could help close Ladder's valuation gap:

Upside Catalysts

Liquidity and Capacity to Play Offense

Ladder has a strong balance sheet and significant capacity to increase leverage, enabling management to deploy capital one-sidedly if opportunity arises. Ladder could write new loans at spreads that LADR would find attractive, or acquire assets at favorable prices while weaker competitors pull back or retrench. I believe management could be opportunistic in returning capital (share repurchase or special dividend) to shareholders if the market continues to discount Ladder's valuation to intrinsic value. In April, Ladder's Board increased share repurchase authorization to $100 million, giving management the discretion to repurchase shares if LADR trades at a persistent discount to intrinsic value. Combined with the already generous ~8%–9% dividend yield, this creates a strong "shareholder yield" backdrop. This could help unlock value if executed by management.

Improvement in General CRE Market Sentiment

Any improvement in CRE market sentiment or any future Federal Reserve rate cuts (which would reduce refinancing pressure) could be upside catalysts. This would help Ladder's CRE asset values and make investors feel more comfortable with the stock.

Recovery in Specific CRE Segments

If certain CRE segments experience a recovery, Ladder may benefit from it. For example, if office CRE becomes more stable, Ladder could see its asset values increase.

Operational Execution

Ladder executes well on its pipeline of originations and asset management, leading to better-than-expected financial performance.

Dividend Sustainability Stress-Test

I look at Ladder’s cash flow and leverage metrics to assess dividend safety. TTM cash flow from operations was ~$134 million versus common dividends of ~$118 million, implying high-80s FCF payout ratio (reasonable for a REIT that needs to pay out 90% of taxable income). Ladder’s net debt/EBITDA is moderate for its financing strategy (debt of $2.77 B offset by cash of $0.48 B, net ~$2.3 B; debt/equity ~1.8x).

Meanwhile, interest coverage remains reasonable; in 2024 Ladder achieved $153.9 M in distributable earnings and had ~$180 M interest expense, implying ~1.8x coverage of interest cost by operating profits. Altman Z-score ~7.5 indicates virtually no bankruptcy risk given Ladder’s working capital and equity buffers.

Lastly, Ladder’s 10-yr dividend track record in context: the 2020 cut (40% reduction, >1 utils cut during the COVID crisis) was also an interruption. The annual dividend has been raised from $0.80 in 2021 to $0.92 currently. Ladder’s conservative credit underwriting and relative preference for cash retention (Ladder’s retained earnings remains negative from prior payouts) suggest dividend preservation is a primary operating goal. Except in the case of an acute CRE credit crisis, I believe Ladder’s dividend appears to be well covered by cash flows and a durable capital structure.

Underwriting Discipline and Structural Edge

What makes Ladder worth owning, in my view, is the thinking that goes into how the business is built—not just the end results. This is a lender that exercises restraint where others chase volume. Most of its loans are first-lien positions with conservative loan-to-value profiles, held directly on balance sheet rather than packaged and sold. That decision alone says a lot about how the firm manages exposure. Instead of reaching for yield, Ladder sticks to income-producing, cash-flowing assets it can underwrite with conviction. The portfolio is intentionally diversified—loans, securities, equity stakes—which gives the company more flexibility across cycles. Management has consistently sidestepped obvious trouble spots like transitional office or speculative ground-up projects, and that shows up in how well book value has held up while many peers stumbled.

There's a clear operating philosophy here: don't stretch, stay liquid, and don’t chase what you don’t understand. I think that mindset matters. It’s not just about hitting a target yield—it’s about how those returns are generated and what’s being risked in the process. In Ladder’s case, what you’re getting is a conservative, internally aligned team that’s seen several market cycles and continues to manage as if the next one is always around the corner. That’s the kind of platform I’m comfortable owning through uncertainty.

Ladder Capital’s High-Quality Assets

Ladder’s conservative underwriting is evident in the standout properties it finances in top markets. For example, Ladder provided a $55 million loan to refinance 17 West in Miami Beach – a mixed-use retail and residential project anchored by the city’s first Trader Joe’s grocery store. The 185,000 sq. ft. property spans a full city block just off the high-traffic Lincoln Road retail corridor and includes ground-floor retail (also leased to tenants like Truist Bank and Verizon) with 23 loft-style apartments above. Ladder also financed Wynwood Walk with a $33.5 million loan – a four-building retail portfolio in Miami’s trendy Wynwood Arts District. Both Miami deals underscore Ladder’s focus on necessity-based and high-foot-traffic retail assets in Florida’s strongest submarkets, reflecting a selective strategy that favors well-located, income-stable properties.

DDM Valuation

To gauge Ladder Capital's intrinsic value, I ran a Dividend Discount Model ("DDM") analysis. Ladder's annualized dividend is $0.92 per share, based on the current $0.23 quarterly dividend, and has been more than covered by distributable earnings. Assuming LADR maintains the $0.92 dividend and can deliver a modest ~2% long-term growth rate in dividends to reflect potential incremental dividend growth as earnings scale, I then estimate a reasonable cost of equity to discount future dividends. Considering LADR's small-cap/higher yield REIT profile (but tempered by its investment-grade credit rating), I applied an expected return/cost of equity of ~10% in my base case. Using the constant-dividend-growth DDM formula (Dividend Per Share / (Cost of Equity - Growth Rate)), this yields an intrinsic value of roughly $11.5–$12.0 per share (e.g., $0.92 / (10% - 2%) = $11.5).

Adjusting the model for a more bullish scenario, like reducing the cost of equity to 9% or increasing the long-term growth rate to 3.0%, pushes the DDM valuation to ~$13 per share. Under my suite of DDM scenarios, Ladder's stock should be in the low-teens range — roughly 20-25% above the stock's recent ~$10 level. Stated another way, a dividend-oriented valuation framework implies LADR's current valuation is unduly discounting the company's long-term cash generation. This analysis highlights that Ladder Capital's stock price is disconnected from its underlying fundamentals, and the anticipated upside vs. the current dividend is highly likely to be increasingly recognized by investors.

Risks to My Bullish Thesis

Key risks to my bullish thesis include a deterioration in commercial real estate fundamentals or credit markets beyond current expectations.

Macro Headwinds

If CRE asset values decline further, for example if a deeper slump in office property demand occurs or higher cap rates prevail, Ladder could face loan losses or mark-to-market impairments that erode its book value. A protracted recession or liquidity crunch could also widen credit spreads, raising Ladder’s funding costs and pressuring its interest spread – noting that Ladder’s investment-grade credit rating is still one notch shy of official IG, so any rating downgrade or negative outlook could increase borrowing costs.

Earnings Volatility

Ladder’s quarterly distributable earnings have some lumpiness. In Q1 2025, for instance, its EPS missed forecasts due to the timing of loan originations and securitization gains. If loan repayments continue outpacing new originations for longer than expected, earnings could undershoot the dividend, forcing higher payout ratios or limiting growth. Additionally, while Ladder has minimal exposure to mark-to-market financing, a sharp rise in credit defaults in its loan book could still hit earnings and test dividend coverage.

These risks, however, are mitigated in part by Ladder’s strong liquidity and diversified portfolio (loans, securities, and equity investments across property types), as well as management’s demonstrated agility in past cycles. I believe the overall thesis – a well-capitalized REIT trading below intrinsic value with a sustainable high yield – remains intact unless an extreme downside scenario unfolds.

Concluding Thoughts

Ladder Capital stands out in the commercial mortgage REIT space for its combination of high-yield and prudent risk management. I rate LADR a Buy, as the stock’s ~9% dividend yield is amply covered (~70–75% payout of earnings) and the shares trade at a discount to the ~$13+ per share economic book value. With insiders owning over 11% of the company and a history of conservative underwriting, I believe investors are well-aligned with management to realize value. In my view, Ladder’s current valuation offers an attractive entry point to lock in a durable high-income stream, with potential upside from gradual dividend growth and accretive buybacks driving total returns in the years ahead.

data.title: Diamond Hill Intermediate Bond Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index returned +2.78% in Q1 2025. This performance marks the best start to a calendar year since 2023 and was characterized by a strong start that faded as more news emerged about tariffs. The January momentum was quickly derailed by a combination of the release of DeepSeek’s AI model, questioning the sustainability of the tech market run over the past two years, and the beginning of what would become the most disruptive impact to the financial markets since the financial crisis, tariffs.

As the quarter progressed, volatility in the Treasury market became the name of the game as the markets reacted to economic news about inflation, consumer sentiment and the labor market, and the implementation/cancellation/reintroduction of tariffs. Consider that the 10-year Treasury yield moved higher or lower by seven basis points (BPs) or more on roughly 22% of the trading days in the first quarter, compared to roughly 17% for all of 2024. The 10-year Treasury yield finished only slightly lower in January, while February saw the yield compress from 4.54% to 4.21% as the aforementioned concerns grew and investors sought the safety of the Treasury market. March experienced some intra-month volatility but finished the month at nearly the same level as it began.

The Merrill Lynch Option Volatility Estimate (MOVE Index) tracks volatility in the US Treasury market to provide insight into the fixed income markets. The MOVE Index utilizes options-pricing models based on a weighted average of option probabilities to reflect expectations for future volatility. Exhibit 1 illustrates the shifts in the MOVE Index and the 10-year US Treasury yield since the beginning of the year.

The Federal Reserve remained squarely on the sidelines as inflation proved stubborn during the quarter and the labor market continued to show strength, adding an average of 152,000 jobs per month during the quarter. The federal funds rate remained in the 4.25%-4.50% range, and the Fed used the two meetings in Q1 to reinforce its data dependency approach. The second meeting on 19 March saw the release of the most recent dot plot, and while there were some slight shifts in projections, expectations for the future path of rates remained at two 25-bps reductions by year-end. Fed Chair Jerome Powell cited the uncertainty surrounding the implementation and impact of tariffs on the US and global economy as one of the reasons for holding the line on rates. Fed fund futures were a bit ahead of the Federal Reserve by the end of the quarter, pricing in roughly three 25-bps reductions by the end of the year.

And what of the two main focuses of the Federal Reserve, inflation and the labor market? Core inflation reports during the quarter were a bit of a rollercoaster, starting the year at 3.2%, peaking at 3.3% in January before reversing course in February (3.1%) and March (2.8%). It should be noted that the backward-looking inflation report has yet to account for the potential impact of tariffs, and the recent slowdown could just be a bump in the road.

The labor market began 2025 on a slower note compared to the strong finish of 2024, which saw 323,000 jobs added in December. In January, 111,000 jobs were added, followed by 117,000 in February and 228,000 in March. This brought the first quarter’s total to nearly 500,000 jobs, with an average monthly gain of 152,000. The uncertainty around the impact of tariffs appears to be keeping the Fed in pause mode for the foreseeable future, reserving the right to adjust as the markets require.

While concerns about tariffs gripped the financial markets, data regarding the consumer moved to the forefront with historically poor readings on everything from consumer confidence to inflation expectations. The University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index, which collects consumer attitudes and expectations data, spent the first part of 2025 in a downward trend. After starting the year at 74.0, which is slightly below the average level of 81.7 since 2000, each subsequent reading pushed lower as concerns about the impact of tariffs and the stubbornness of inflation grew. The index finished the quarter at 57.0, with a considerable drop in April’s report to 50.8, its lowest reading since 2022 and the second lowest on record. Consumer inflation expectations for the next year grew throughout the quarter, from 2.8% at the end of 2024 to 5.0% in March and 6.7% in April (as of 11 April 2025).

Portfolio Performance & Positioning

As illustrated in Exhibit 2, the biggest move in the Treasury curve occurred in February as investors sought the safety of Treasurys in the face of economic uncertainty. After remaining essentially unchanged month over month in January, the 10- year Treasury yield rallied from 4.58% to 4.24% in February, the biggest shift lower since July 2024, when the 10-year yield dropped 36.6 bps month over month. The quarter’s final month saw the curve steepen as the shorter end rallied and the 30-year moved higher by 8 bps.

The February rally delivered the best performance for the Treasury market (+2.16% as measured by the Bloomberg US Treasury Index) since July 2024 (+2.19%). As rates decrease, bond prices increase, and the longer the duration, the more impactful the move in interest rates. With the entire curve moving lower during the quarter, longer duration Treasurys delivered the strongest performance with the 30-year bellwether Treasury returning +4.28% and the 10-year returning +3.99%. But the shorter end held up well, with the 2-year Treasury bellwether up +1.59%.

Exhibit 2 — US Treasury Yields (%)

Treasury Yields 31-Dec-24 31-Jan-25 28-Feb-25 31-Mar-25 QoQ Change
3M 4.23 4.2 4.2 4.21 -0.02
6M 4.13 4.16 4.14 4.08 -0.05
1Y 4.16 4.17 4.08 4.03 -0.13
2Y 4.25 4.22 3.99 3.89 -0.36
5Y 4.38 4.36 4.03 3.96 -0.42
10Y 4.58 4.58 4.24 4.23 -0.35
30Y 4.78 4.83 4.51 4.59 -0.19

Source: FRED.

The portfolio’s duration began the year at roughly 95% of the benchmark’s duration and moved slightly lower by the end of the first quarter (93.4%). Although the portfolio’s overall duration is shorter than the benchmark’s, its Treasury allocation

duration is longer than the benchmark’s Treasury duration. This positioning helps counterbalance the significant underweight in Treasurys from a percentage standpoint. Impact from the overall portfolio duration relative to the benchmark was a slight positive during the first quarter of the year, as the longer duration posture relative to the benchmark was more than enough to offset the impact of an underweight position.

The Bloomberg US Intermediate Corporate Bond Index returned 2.27% during the first quarter, despite the spread widening that occurred throughout the quarter, as the index option-adjusted spread level moved from 71.1 bps to 83.4 bps. As illustrated in Exhibit 3, February’s performance was the biggest contributor during the quarter, and the sector received a helping hand from the rally in Treasurys (price impact).

Performance for intermediate corporates by credit quality saw BBB-rated bonds gain +2.21%, A-rated up +2.38%, AA-rated returning +2.24% and AAA-rated up +1.95%.

Issuance in the investment grade corporate space remains strong, with more than $500 billion in new deals coming into the market in Q1. This level is well ahead of the 4-year moving average ($453 billion in the first quarter), though expectations are for a sizable slowdown as volatility continues to plague the market. Our portfolio’s underweight relative to the benchmark in the corporate sector was a slight detractor to the portfolio’s relative performance during the first quarter.

Exhibit 3 — Bloomberg US Intermediate Corporate Index Return Components (%)

Price Coupon Total Return
January 0.27 0.35 0.62
February 1.02 0.35 1.37
March -0.08 0.35 0.27
Total 1.2 1.06 2.27

Source: Bloomberg.

The securitized sector (as measured by the Bloomberg US Securitized index) was up 3.00% in Q1, a swift turnaround from the prior quarter’s downturn. While the securitized sector in the portfolio trailed the benchmark’s sector return, the significant overweight in the portfolio relative to poorer performing sectors (corporates, Treasurys) positively contributed to relative performance during the quarter.

The residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) component of the benchmark dominates the securitized sector from a weighting standpoint, and the portfolio’s allocation to this part of the market outperformed during the quarter, contributing to relative performance.

The ability to invest outside of the benchmark in areas of the market that provide similar risk profiles to benchmark-eligible securities was the key to performance in Q1. Consider the overall securitized sector performance: the portfolio’s allocation was up 2.85% in Q1 while the benchmark’s allocation was up +3.00%. Within subsectors of the portfolio, agency RMBS was the best-performing, generating a +3.31% return in the quarter. The shorter duration asset-backed securities (ABS) within the portfolio returned +2.14% compared to the benchmark’s allocation return of +1.53%, fueled by security selection in both auto ABS and consumer unsecured ABS1. The portfolio’s allocation to collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) withstood the interest rate volatility in the market better than the index-eligible agency RMBS, contributing to overall relative performance in Q1. Overall, security selection and non-benchmark allocations contributed positively to performance during the quarter.

The portfolio continues to search for opportunities in the marketplace while maintaining a conservative risk profile relative to the index.

1Data is shown gross of fees and should be viewed in conjunction with the net of fee returns included in this document.

Bonds rated AAA, AA, A and BBB are considered investment grade.

ICE BAML MOVE Index is a measure of US interest rate volatility that tracks the movement in US Treasury yield volatility implied by current prices of one-month over-the-counter options on 2-year, 5-year, 10-year and 30-year Treasurys.

Diamond Hill Capital Management, Inc. (DHCM) is an investment adviser registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission and a wholly owned subsidiary of Diamond Hill Investment Group, Inc.; registration does not imply a certain level of skill or training. DHCM provides investment management services to individuals and institutions through mutual funds, separately managed accounts, collective investment trusts, a private fund, a closed-end interval fund and other pooled vehicles including subadvised funds and model delivery programs. A complete list and description of all composites and policies for valuing investments, calculating performance and preparing GIPS reports is available upon request. To receive a complete list and description of all Diamond Hill composites and/or a GIPS® report, contact Scott Stapleton at 614.255.3329, sstapleton@diamond-hill.com or 325 John H. McConnell Blvd., Suite 200, Columbus, OH 43215. A list of broad distribution pooled funds is available upon request. In addition, a list of limited distribution pooled fund descriptions is available upon request. The Intermediate Bond Composite is comprised of discretionary, fee-paying, non-wrap accounts managed according to the firm's Intermediate Bond fixed income strategy. The strategy's investment objective is to maximize total return consistent with the preservation of capital. The strategy typically invests in a diversified portfolio of investment grade, intermediate, fixed income securities, including bonds, debt securities and other similar US dollar-denominated instruments issued by various US public- or private-sector entities, by non-US corporations or US affiliates of non-US corporations or by non-US governments or their agencies and instrumentalities. The portfolio may invest a significant portion of its assets in asset- backed, mortgage-related and mortgage-backed securities at the discretion of Diamond Hill Capital Management, Inc. (the "Adviser"). The portfolio will typically maintain an average portfolio duration within 20% of the Bloomberg Intermediate US Aggregate Bond Index duration. As of 30 September 2024, there is no minimum account size. Prior to this date, the minimum account size was $50 million. The composite results reflect the reinvestment of dividends, capital gains and other earnings when appropriate. Composite returns and benchmark returns are presented gross of withholding taxes on dividends, interest income and capital gains. Returns are calculated using US Dollars. Net returns are calculated by reducing the gross returns by the highest stated fee in the composite fee schedule. Only transaction costs are deducted from gross of fees returns. Prior to 30 September 2022, actual fees were used in calculating net returns. All net returns were changed retroactively to reflect the highest fee in the composite fee schedule. The Bloomberg US Intermediate Aggregate Bond Index is the benchmark. The index measures the performance of investment grade, fixed-rate taxable bond market and includes government and corporate bonds, agency mortgage-backed, asset-backed and commercial mortgage-backed securities (agency and non-agency) with a maturity greater than 1 year and less than 10 years. The index is unmanaged, includes net reinvested dividends, does not reflect fees or expenses (which would lower the return) and is not available for direct investment. Index data source: Bloomberg Index Services Limited. See diamond- Diamond Hill - Disclosures for a full copy of the disclaimer. The performance data quoted represents past performance; past performance does not guarantee future results. GIPS® is a registered trademark of CFA Institute. CFA Institute does not endorse or promote this organization, nor does it warrant the accuracy or quality of the content contained herein. Analytics provided by The Yield Book® Software.

The views expressed are those of Diamond Hill as of 31 March 2025 and are subject to change without notice. These opinions are not intended to be a forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results or investment advice. Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AT&T's Dividend Yield Gap With 10-Year Treasury Yield Increases Rapidly
data.description:
AT&T (NYSE:T) stock has been able to weather the recent tariff volatility relatively well. The latest earnings result also showed revenue beating estimates by $264 million, which has helped the sentiment. However, AT&T’s dividend yield is now below 4% and there is a big gap with the 10-year treasury yield. This will limit the attraction of the stock for investors seeking good, steady income. The potential of a dividend hike is currently limited, with the management showing a greater inclination towards buybacks. There are few growth levers, and most of the upside from subscriber growth has likely been priced in by the bull run of the last few quarters. In the previous article, it was mentioned that AT&T's valuation could be close to its peak.

AT&T’s fiber revenue has reported 19% YoY growth, which is quite good, but the overall consumer wireline YoY growth has been a mere 3%. The fiber revenue is also only 7% of the total revenue base, which will limit its ability to move the overall revenue needle for AT&T in the near term. AT&T’s net debt-to-adj EBITDA has declined from 2.94X in Q1 2024 to 2.63X in Q1 2025, which is good progress. The forward PE ratio for the fiscal year ending Dec 2027 is 11.33 which is modest, but we could see elevated capex layout in the next few years.

Dividend yield gap with 10-year treasury increases

AT&T’s dividend yield is now at 3.9% compared with 10-year treasury which is close to 4.5%. This is a massive gap in yield, and it will limit how many new income investors might invest at the current price.

In the above chart, we can see that the gap in AT&T's dividend yield and the 10 year treasury rate has declined rapidly in the last few quarters. Since the Great Recession in 2008, it is for the first time that AT&T's dividend yield has been significantly below the 10-year treasury yield.

The management has also mentioned earlier that they would start a buyback program. This will reduce the possibility of any big dividend hike in the near term. The buybacks give the management a more flexible approach to shareholder return. If there are any headwinds, the management could limit the buybacks for a few quarters or even a year or two. On the other hand, any reduction in dividend yield causes negative sentiment on Wall Street and most of the management of mature companies like AT&T use dividend cut as a last resort.

Figure: Recent earnings numbers of AT&T. Source: Seeking Alpha

The recent earnings results were quite good, and the stock performed well during the volatility due to trade tensions in the last few months. However, the upside for the stock seems limited after the recent bull run. Most of the tailwinds from higher subscriber growth have likely been priced in. It is also very important to note the investment horizon which is preferable for AT&T.

A buy-and-hold strategy could underperform significantly

Most of the bullish comments for AT&T mention a buy and hold strategy and reinvesting the dividends. However, it should be noted that AT&T stock has never performed well in terms of total returns when compared to broader S&P total returns for more than five-year period. This trend can be seen for over 30 years and has been reflected during the recessions and bull period.

In the past 5 years, AT&T's total returns have significantly underperformed S&P 500, despite the recent bull run of the last few quarters.

In the above chart, we can see the total returns between 2008-2013. Even during the recession, AT&T's stock has not been a great performer, despite the recession-proof nature of the industry. Hence, it lacks the long-term momentum of growth stocks, while not giving the advantage of defensive stocks.

AT&T stock is already close to its all-time high with a very low dividend yield. This further reduces the benefit as an income-generating safe stock. A buy and hold strategy at the current price might deliver significant underperformance to S&P 500 when we look at a time frame of more than five years.

Positives need to be carefully analysed

AT&T’s management has mentioned the strong performance of the company in the fiber business. In the recent quarter, the fiber revenue showed 19% YoY growth and reached $2.1 billion. Double-digit growth in this segment has been reported for the past few quarters. However, fiber revenue is a part of the consumer wireline revenue base and this segment has not shown a strong growth momentum. In the recent quarter, the consumer wireline business revenue was $3.5 billion, compared to $3.4 billion in the year-ago quarter. Hence, fiber revenue is probably canabalizing other revenue streams of the consumer wireline segment. It should also be noted that fiber revenue contributes only 7% to the overall revenue base, which was $30.6 billion in the recent quarter. It is unlikely that fiber revenue alone might be a major tailwind for revenue and ARPU growth for AT&T for at least the next few years.

Figure: Recent Fiber revenue growth of AT&T. Source: Company Filings

AT&T’s management has focused on reducing the net debt. They have kept the dividends under control and limited capex in order to reduce the net debt. In the recent quarter, the net debt declined to $119.1 billion from $128.7 billion in the year-ago quarter. The net debt-to-adj. EBITDA ratio has also declined to 2.63X in Q1 2025 from 2.94X in Q1 2024. The reduction in debt and leverage in the last few quarters has probably been a bigger bullish trend for AT&T compared to the increase in subscribers. However, the upside from debt reduction has likely been priced in, and the company mentioned that they would soon reach the 2.5X net debt-to-adj. EBITDA target and commence share buybacks from second quarter.

Figure: Debt reduction of AT&T. Source: Company Filings

Can buybacks work their magic?

For a mature company like AT&T, the revenue growth potential is limited. The forward revenue growth forecast is of a mere 1%-3% for the next few years. However, the company can still deliver EPS growth by making massive buybacks. The management has already announced the intention to start buybacks soon, but we would need to see the capital allocated for this program. The consensus EPS estimate shows 7.79% YoY growth for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 and another 10.3% for the fiscal year ending Dec 2027.

Figure: Forward EPS growth forecast. Source: Seeking Alpha

However, the recent $5.75 billion deal with Lumen shows that the management might still divert resources towards acquisitions. This has not helped AT&T in the last decade. Hopefully, the current management will be more prudent in its acquisitions. But there is still very little wiggle room for the company. AT&T spends almost half of its FCF towards dividends, and any major acquisition will leave little option to further reduce debt or make share repurchases. In addition, the current price and modest dividend yield further reduces the attraction of AT&T stock.

Investor Takeaway

AT&T’s dividend yield shows a massive gap with 10-year treasury yield, which can keep income investors away. A buy and hold strategy has not shown good results for AT&T stock in any five-year-plus investment horizon. It is highly likely that AT&T stock will find it difficult to beat the S&P 500 over the next few years, especially as the current price is already quite high.

The fiber business is showing good YoY growth, but it still contributes only 7% of the total revenue base. The fiber business has also not helped consumer wireline segment growth, which grew by a mere 3% YoY. AT&T’s management has mentioned about starting share repurchases in the second quarter, but there is very little wiggle room for the company to invest significantly in buybacks. New acquisitions could also reduce the resources available for buybacks. While the forward PE ratio is modest, the future returns potential looks limited, making the stock a Hold.

data.title: PTC: Margin Strength Meets Valuation Reset
data.description:
PTC Inc. (NASDAQ:PTC) has a diversified software portfolio spanning CAD, PLM, IoT, AR and service lifecycle management, with a target market primarily in heavy engineering industries. Performance metrics are solid, supported by its high margin recurring revenue model and good enterprise adoption. While it operates in highly competitive markets, facing pressure from giants like Autodesk, Siemens and Dassault, PTC maintains a moderate organic moat particularly in industrial business' product development ecosystems.

Fundamentals are Sound

There are no concerns about PTC's topline with reasonable revenue growth reported in Q2 2025. Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) was in double-digit growth territory. Guidance, however, is cautious with a downward revision from 9.5% ARR earlier to 8% now at the midpoint. That is more to do with the macro pressures ahead than business fundamentals.

PTC's revenue growth is not stalled. The numbers need to be looked at from the lens of its life stage and placement. For a vertical SaaS player who is in mid to late-stage maturity, these revenue growth numbers are strong. I am evaluating PTC as a defensive IT play, where other peers like Autodesk and Dassault show ~10% is a very good growth benchmark. Growth numbers do fluctuate across quarters. PTC's growth rate, after guidance cuts, is reasonably good.

PTC's strategic push into SaaS like offerings like Onshape and Arena positions it to benefit from long-term shifts toward cloud native product development. These newer platforms may be a source of optionality not valued into prices, particularly given its margin expansion and TAM growth potential.

Premium Valuations No More

PTC was trading at a premium valuation against an established competitor like Autodesk not so long ago, but now is at a discount, trading at a forward PE of ~27x. The recent contraction could be due to the cautious guidance for 2025. But note the expanding margins (subsequent section) despite a moderating revenue. ARR, even with the moderated guidance, looks healthy. Any ARR acceleration will push up PTC share prices.

PTC's valuation now becomes all the more lucrative if we look at the FCF yield versus Autodesk. Clearly, PTC is converting a higher portion of its revenue into cash, led by operational efficiencies and healthy subscription penetration.

Peer Comparisons - PTC's Margin Advantage

PTC's operating margins are not only higher than Dassault and Autodesk, it is still growing while competitors have stagnated. How far the margins can grow will be dependent on how much more SaaS like pivots PTC can manage in the future. In that regard, the competitors may have more room to play, as PTC's recurring revenue as a % of total revenue already looks heavily SaaS like. But even if the margins stay where they are, it is far superior to competition. With lower valuations now and PTC looking well poised to deliver better EPS, share prices have a lot of upside room to run. The combination of expanding healthy margins at decent growth is a textbook defensive IT buy which has topline room to run.

R&D and Pipeline

PTC operates with a leaner R&D model compared to Autodesk, spending around 20% of revenue on R&D. This without losing competitive edge on its innovation pipeline. Its narrower focus on industrials allows it to deliver more revenue per R&D dollar ($5 vs Autodesk's ~$4).

In terms of pipeline readiness for future product expansions, PTC has already integrated AI features into the Creo CAD platform (design assistance and generative modeling). PTC is also expanding the Windchill analytics stack and Vuforia's AR capabilities, supporting use cases like predictive maintenance and technician training. Research and capability building is more targeted and agile than larger peers like Autodesk.

Risks to the Thesis

While the fundamentals remain solid, a few data-backed risks deserve attention. PTC's ARR growth has slowed from ~16% in FY 2022 (constant currency basis) to ~10% in Q2 FY 2025, with full-year guidance now trimmed to ~8%. This indicates potential demand saturation or macro linked deferrals in industrial software spends. PTC is additionally always exposed to regional CapEx cycles. Competitive pricing pressure from Autodesk and Dassault also limits upside unless PTC maintains aggressive margin discipline.

Can Buy PTC

Summarizing the risks for a Buy in PTC now, I see limited room for SaaS upside unless new offering come into play. Cyclical CapEx downtimes and spending cuts are relevant now given macro headwinds. ARR growth is good but decelerating and guidance is further cautious within reasonable limits for 2025.

The supporting facts for a Buy are many. PTC's prime moat is not its product capability, but its industry focused foothold, that becomes sticky over time. This focus also helps drive leaner product development and enhancements compared to larger peers like Autodesk. The pipeline of upcoming products and AI enhancements give potential margin expansion opportunities, which is already best in class and expanding. To top it all is the valuation discount we see now, giving an opportunity to bet on EPS growth and valuation expansion to provide double support to share prices.

I rate PTC as a Buy for long-term investors, especially those seeking tech exposure outside hyperscalers or consumer tech. PTC offers a defensive, high-quality opportunity with asymmetric upside if ARR reaccelerates after a temporary slowdown. A conservative case sees EPS growing to ~$6 by FY 2026 (compared to consensus estimates of ~$6.12 by FY 2025), and PTC re-rating modestly from 27× to 35× near the top of its own historical highs after macro easing and ARR rebounds. That yields a price target of ~$210 in 12–18 months, ~20% upside. Downside remains limited given PTC's defensive characteristics and strong FCF.

data.title: LVHI: Diversified, Defensive And Delivering
data.description:
The Franklin International Low Volatility High Dividend Index ETF (BATS:LVHI) appears to be a decent returning low volatile promise with international diversification and generous dividend income. International markets are overall correlated to the US markets, but the correlation has become lower over the past year or so. That coupled with the geopolitical uncertainties, including the US' own troubles, makes a strong case for LVHI on paper. Below is a correlation graph of S&P 500 (proxied as SPY) and an India ETF. Strong decoupling is evident in 2025, but overall directional broad correlation (return to mean behavior is not ruled out long term - all previous deviations have finally merged).

Strong Diversification Principles

The example used for global diversification (emerging market like India versus a developed market like Japan) is not probably applicable for LVHI as LVHI mostly picks mixed market cap stocks from developed markets ex-US. Currency risks are taken out of the equation as appreciate currency hedges are taken as part of all international holdings. LVHI starts from the MSCI World ex‑US IMI Index and applies filters on earnings and sustainable dividend expectations. Factors are combined in a way to weigh in price volatility. Higher interest rate regions also drop out. What remains is a list of low volatile high dividend plays from developed regions (lower interest rates as well). Diversification is further ensured by capping any holdings to be a maximum of 2.5%. There are caps on country exposure (15%), sectoral exposure (25%) and even geography/region exposure (50%). REIT investments are allowed up to 15%. All in all, a very well diversified approach, where the top 10 holdings cannot account for more than 25% - a refreshing changed from today's highly concentrated bets in most funds.

The diversification is very visible in allocation patterns. UK, Japan and Canada are the top three heavyweight countries in the mix. The top holdings do appear to be some of the most defensive and dividend strong companies I know. The market cap distribution shows exposure across, but comforting dominant large cap exposure for a defensive fund.

Income and NAV Preservation

The dividend yield is stable and clocks 4-5% yield on average. There are periods of lower payouts, so dividend consistency is not the best, but nothing concerning. Provided the underlying capital is growing well. If the dividend payouts are eroding NAV, that could be a red flag.

The NAV also ticks the right boxes. First of all, NAV is not eroding over time. Many income plans are guilty of negligible NAV appreciation (some even erode aggressively) to accommodate payouts. Since LVHI is not an income plan, it passes on dividends received directly as income for investors. It doesn't do aggressive payout damages to NAV. IF NAV does not increase over time, dividends stagnate and, when inflation adjusted, lead to lower value in the long run (even if yield does not drop). The NAV, in the case of LVHI is actually growing at a CAGR of ~3.5% since inception, which is great for a defensive portfolio with a 4-5% payout rate.

Total Returns Performance

Total returns for an ETF like LVHI is not a qualifying criteria. Drawdowns and correlation are. But it could be criteria to rate it as a strong buy, if good. In a common period of existence for the past 8 years or so, a comparison with core US equity (SPY) shows a ~10.5% CAGR for LVHI vs ~15.5% for SPY. Standalone, I am more than fine with the 10.5% CAGR total return for LVHI. The difference against US Equity (SPY, not the most growth oriented) is not a dealbreaker but significant. This difference could be indeed acceptable if the hedging requirements of LVHI are met.

As a Hedge

To understand whether LVHI's core proposition of uncorrelated markets providing effective hedges, holds in principle (historically), we will look at the COVID crash, 2022-23 downturn and the recent tariff sell-off. The COVID crash is a global impact period, and I am expecting not much difference due to geographical diversification - maybe a bit of outperformance due to LVHI's defensive portfolio. And the graph below upholds that. There is nothing to pick in a fast and global crash.

Differences are more expected in the recent tariff sell-off, which was part global, but also impacted different countries differently. When I plot total returns, this expectation is reflected. In fact, for some part of that sell-off, LVHI actually was positive while SPY was on a downtrend. Shows LVHI does show its hedging capabilities in sell-offs that are region-specific.

I expect the most difference in the 2022-23 interest rate sell-off. The graph below does show how LVHI was almost in positive territory right through the trough for US markets. Overall, the performance differential with respect to SPY is more than made up by LVHI's hedging capabilities. Far more digestible drawdowns unless there's a global crash.

Strong Buy Despite Performance Gaps

I did have some concerns about the performance gap for LVHI vs SPY. But this is very, very acceptable when looked at from a defensive income generating perspective. LVHI is as market agnostic an income plan as can realistically be. Preserves optionality of upsides, hedges efficiently against significant drawdowns in most cases, with a high and stable yield for a dividend focused ETF.

I rate LVHI a strong buy as a satellite holding for your core portfolio. The case becomes even stronger when you see things from the bull run perspective in the last 8 years overall. Performance gaps will be much lower if markets even decelerate from here on. Macro pressures and geopolitical risks aside.
